rationales.stimulation: He savors "the routine of it" and says "the route is automatic," showing comfort with sameness rather than seeking novelty or challenge.
rationales.security: He feels good about "same walk, same coat, same apartment" and values that "nothing is asking me to figure anything out," tying relief to predictability and stability.
A casual mention that the landlord "might sell the building" leaves him "staring at the wall" for twenty minutes, showing how strongly housing uncertainty hits him.

rationales.stimulation: He rejected a "nicer place" because this one felt "more predictable" and resents advice to "just deal with it when it happens," signaling aversion to uncertainty and change.
rationales.security: He chose the apartment because it "felt locked in" and keeps "running the numbers" once that stability is threatened, showing strong focus on predictable housing and finances.
He knows the higher premium is "eroding the margin I built into my budget" but still lets it "auto-renew and deal with it later," neglecting a clear step to protect his financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: He says he is "wondering why I didn't just ask" about the funding details and only nodded along, showing reluctance to assert his own needs or seek clarity.
rationales.security: He frames the conference as a "maybe-partially-funded trip" while his "housing situation is already shaky," focusing on the financial downside over the opportunity.
He spends the evening combing listings and making a spreadsheet because the developer talks mean "the clock is ticking," showing active effort to regain housing stability.
rationales.benevolence: He hides the problem from his mom because "she worries and then she offers money she doesn't really have," prioritizing her wellbeing over his urge to unload the stress.

rationales.achievement: He submitted the chapter "two months ahead of the internal deadline" after blocking out time every morning, reflecting disciplined effort to perform well.
rationales.security: He says he "can't function when something is hanging over me unfinished" and values that "the plan actually held," linking peace of mind to order and predictability.
He chooses the den because Priya is "consistent," the rent can fit his budget, and the arrangement lets him "know where I'm living in June" and finally sleep.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers over a fresh slice with hot Milo while "the kitchen smelled good for hours" and says "It was nice," centering simple sensory pleasure.
She gives up the CNY market they had "been planning for two weeks" and dismisses her own enjoyment with "what am I going to say ... I want to go shopping?"
rationales.benevolence: She agrees to watch the grandkids "obviously" because "She's my daughter," putting family needs ahead of her own plans.

rationales.hedonism: Even when "the children were fine," she feels guilty for "maybe ten minutes" on the sofa and gives up the "long bath," blocking herself from rest and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the whole day caring for the grandkids and keeps monitoring whether they need fruit, tidying, or water, staying oriented toward their needs.

rationales.hedonism: She knows "a massage would help" and that lying down with a heat pad would help, but denies herself both and "kept going" through pain.
rationales.tradition: Her choice is shaped by "my mother's voice in my head" saying not to be "so pampered," showing deference to inherited family norms about endurance.

rationales.hedonism: She waits in line just because "I could smell it" and then sits alone enjoying the laksa without thinking about "what was next," prioritizing immediate pleasure.
She wants to enjoy the pastry with tea but ends up "eating the pastry over the sink" while her mind is "three tasks ahead," so pleasure gets crowded out by duty.
rationales.benevolence: She calls Ma first because "she gets anxious if I don't call by 7" and then automatically saves the rest "for the grandkids," prioritizing close family members.
She brings Ma the dried tangerine peel she asked for, adjusts the aircon, adds a blanket, and refills her thermos, consistently tending to a close family member's comfort.

rationales.hedonism: Even though "$38" is affordable, she feels she must "justify it first" and "deserve an hour of someone rubbing my feet," treating pleasure as something she has to earn.
She follows through on the reflexology, says "It was good," and notes it "actually made a difference," showing a concrete choice for comfort and enjoyment even though worry crept in afterward.
